title: The Safavid Order
bullets: The Safavid dynasty began as a mainstream Sunni Sufi brotherhood called the Safaviyya, founded around 1300 by Sheikh Safi al-Din in the small Azerbaijani town of Ardabil — as unlikely an origin for an empire as early-modern history offers.

Sufi orders called tariqas flourished across the Islamic world after the Mongol sack of Baghdad in 1258, filling the vacuum left by the collapsed Abbasid caliphate with local spiritual direction, social organization, and political networks.

The Safaviyya's gradual shift from Sunni to Shia theology, starting in the late fourteenth century, was driven by the order's recruitment of Turcoman nomads whose folk Islam blended Sunni and Shia traditions with pre-Islamic shamanic elements.

The Safavid sheikhs claimed descent from Ali ibn Abi Talib, the Prophet's cousin and son-in-law — a genealogy almost certainly invented in the fifteenth century but central to Safavid legitimacy ever after.

Militarization came under sheikhs Junayd and Haydar in the mid-to-late 1400s; Haydar designed the distinctive twelve-pointed red turban that gave his Turcoman warriors the name Qizilbash, Turkish for 'red-heads.'

The Qizilbash were not merely soldiers for hire: they believed their sheikh was a divinely guided, near-messianic figure, possibly the returning Hidden Imam, and charged into battle convinced they could not be killed.
After Haydar was killed in battle in 1488, a six-year-old Ismail was smuggled to safety by loyal followers and spent five years in hiding before emerging at thirteen to lead an army.

Both the Safavids and the Ottomans — the two empires about to become nemeses — were born from Sufi orders in the chaos of the post-Mongol world and faced the same structural problem of replacing founding tribal armies with personally loyal crown forces.
subtitle: a Sunni Sufi brotherhood in Ardabil becomes a Shia messianic army

title: Ismail and the Shia Revolution
bullets: In 1501, a fourteen-year-old Ismail captured Tabriz with a few thousand Turcoman cavalry and declared himself Shah — the first Persian-language title used by Iranian kings in over eight centuries — and immediately declared Twelver Shia Islam the state religion.
Iran in 1501 was roughly 80–90% Sunni; what Ismail proposed was not religious reform but religious revolution, enforced by state violence applied systematically over decades.

The conversion was run through public humiliation as well as executions: teams of preachers called tabarra'iyyan were sent through conquered cities to curse the first three caliphs, and anyone who failed to join in could be killed on the spot.

There were almost no Twelver Shia scholars inside Iran, so Ismail imported them — paying Arab clerics from Jabal Amil in southern Lebanon and from Bahrain enormous sums to migrate to Qom and Isfahan and train the Iranian clerical class.

The imported Lebanese scholars and their Iranian-born descendants became the founding stock of Iran's Shia clerical establishment, the same lineage that would eventually produce Ayatollah Khomeini and the 1979 Islamic Revolution.

Ismail conquered most of the Iranian plateau within a decade, defeating the Aq Qoyunlu, capturing Baghdad and the Shia holy cities of Najaf and Karbala, and killing the Uzbek leader Muhammad Shaybani at the Battle of Merv in 1510.
After Merv, Ismail had Shaybani's skull encased in gold and made into a drinking cup, claiming the political legacy of Genghis Khan as well as the religious legacy of Ali.
He was twenty-three years old when the conquest of Iran was essentially complete, and the only major power he had not yet faced was the Ottoman Empire to his west.
subtitle: a fourteen-year-old Shah forcibly converts an 80% Sunni country to Shia Islam

title: Chaldiran and the Frontier
bullets: At Chaldiran on August 23, 1514, the Ottoman Sultan Selim I deployed perhaps 12,000 Janissary musketeers and artillery behind a wagon-fort perimeter against a Safavid force of roughly 40,000 lance-and-sword Qizilbash cavalry with no gunpowder weapons.

The Qizilbash charged believing their sheikh's spiritual power made them invincible; the Ottoman muskets and cannons did not care, and the Safavid left wing was destroyed before reaching the Ottoman line.

Ismail was wounded, his horse killed under him, and his harem captured; he recovered Tabriz after the Ottomans withdrew but never led troops in battle again, spending the last decade of his life in mourning and drinking heavily.

The defeat broke the aura of divine invincibility that had powered the Qizilbash, and subsequent Safavid Shahs presented themselves as orthodox Shia monarchs rather than living gods — a shift that made the dynasty more institutionally sustainable.

Tahmasp I, who inherited the throne at ten in 1524 and reigned for fifty-two years, held the dynasty together through three major Ottoman wars and repeated Uzbek invasions, moving the capital from vulnerable Tabriz to inland Qazvin in 1548.

The Shahnameh of Shah Tahmasp, produced at the Tabriz court between 1522 and 1535, contained 258 full-page miniature paintings and weighed roughly 75 pounds — the most ambitious illustrated manuscript in Iranian history.

Tahmasp sent the manuscript as a diplomatic gift to Ottoman Sultan Selim II in 1568; it was eventually broken up by the twentieth-century art market and its surviving pages now hang in museums from New York to Doha.

The brief reign of Ismail II in 1576–77 nearly reversed the Shia conversion, but he was found dead — almost certainly poisoned — demonstrating that in just seventy-five years the Shia clerical establishment had become powerful enough to destroy a Shah who opposed it.
subtitle: Ottoman gunpowder defeats the Qizilbash and fixes the frontier for five centuries

title: Abbas the Great and Isfahan
bullets: Abbas I inherited a collapsing empire in 1588 at age sixteen — Ottomans occupying the west, Uzbeks raiding the east, empty treasury, Qizilbash factions fighting each other — and his first move was signing a humiliating peace with the Ottomans to buy time.

The ghulam revolution replaced the politically unreliable Qizilbash cavalry with a standing army recruited from Caucasian captives — Georgians, Armenians, Circassians — converted, trained in royal palace schools, equipped with muskets and cannons, and answerable solely to the Shah.

The most famous ghulam, Allahverdi Khan — a Georgian boy captured in the Caucasus — rose to commander-in-chief of the Safavid army, led the crucial victory over the Ottomans at Sufiyan in 1605, and built the Si-o-se-pol bridge in Isfahan.

With his rebuilt army Abbas recovered Khorasan from the Uzbeks, recaptured Tabriz and the Caucasus from the Ottomans, took Hormuz from the Portuguese in 1622, and retook Baghdad in 1623 — reversing nearly every loss of the previous century.

Abbas moved the capital to Isfahan in 1598 and built Naqsh-e Jahan Square — roughly twice the size of St. Peter's Square in Rome — with the Shah Mosque, the Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque, the Ali Qapu palace, and the Grand Bazaar as its four anchor buildings.

The Shah Mosque's entrance portal faces the square while the prayer hall must face Mecca, a 45-degree difference; the architects resolved it with a seamless transitional vestibule so smooth that most visitors never notice the turn.

Isfahan grew from about 80,000 people in 1598 to an estimated 500,000 by the 1620s — one of the largest cities in the world — giving rise to the proverb Isfahan nesf-e jahan ast: Isfahan is half the world.

Abbas had personally killed or blinded all his potential heirs to prevent palace coups, which meant the greatest Safavid ruler left the throne to an untested grandson — Safi I — who would begin the long decline.
subtitle: the ghulam revolution, military recovery, and Isfahan as half the world

title: The Shia Clergy and the Isfahan School
bullets: The doctrinal battle between the Akhbari school (follow the Imams' transmitted rulings directly) and the Usuli school (senior scholars may exercise independent reasoning) determined whether the Shia clergy would be a state tool or an independent institutional power — and the Usuli school won.

The Usuli victory created the marja-i taqlid system in which the most senior living mujtahids issue binding rulings that ordinary Shia must follow, a hierarchy with its own legitimacy independent of any political ruler.

Mulla Sadra, the greatest philosopher of the Isfahan school, was driven into rural exile at the village of Kahak by hostile clerics who considered his metaphysics heretical — and wrote most of his major work there, far from the capital that had trained him.

Mir Damad, called 'the Third Teacher' after Aristotle and al-Farabi, and Sheikh Bahai — polymath, mathematician, astronomer, hydraulic engineer, and poet — were the court-favored establishment figures whose work Sadra inherited and radicalized.

Sheikh Bahai, a Lebanese Arab imported under the conversion project, calculated the qibla for the Shah Mosque, designed Isfahan's irrigation network, and wrote standard seminary textbooks in theology, algebra, and astronomy simultaneously.

The shrine of Imam Reza at Mashhad and the seminary city of Qom — both massively expanded under the Safavids — created a specifically Iranian Shia religious geography that did not depend on Ottoman-controlled Najaf and Karbala.

Muhammad Baqir Majlisi, chief religious official of Isfahan in the late seventeenth century, compiled the 110-volume Bihar al-Anwar hadith collection, persecuted Sufi orders, and became the first Shia cleric powerful enough to dictate religious policy to the Shah.

The institutional chain from the seventeenth-century Qom hawza to Ayatollah Khomeini's training to the current Islamic Republic is essentially unbroken — the most important Safavid creation is the one that does not appear on any tourist itinerary.
subtitle: Mulla Sadra, Majlisi, and the clerical hierarchy that outlasted the dynasty

title: Trade, Carpets, and the World
bullets: Abbas I declared a royal monopoly on raw silk export in 1599, rerouting Iranian silk away from Ottoman transit taxes through the Persian Gulf and Caspian routes to European merchants, dramatically increasing the share of sale price that reached the Iranian treasury.

The Armenians of Old Julfa — the most successful silk-trading merchant community in the Caucasus — were forcibly relocated to a new district called New Julfa beside Isfahan in 1604–05, their commercial network effectively nationalized in service of the silk monopoly.

Within a few decades New Julfan Armenian merchants operated trading houses in Venice, Amsterdam, London, Surat, Manila, and Lhasa, making them one of the great early-modern merchant diasporas alongside the Sephardic Jewish and Gujarati Hindu networks.

In 1622, an Anglo-Persian alliance expelled the Portuguese from the island of Hormuz after 115 years of occupation, with English EIC ships landing Iranian troops in exchange for commercial privileges and a share of customs revenues.

The Ardabil Carpet, commissioned around 1539–40 for the family shrine at Ardabil, measured 34 by 17 feet with approximately 25 million individual hand-tied knots by master weaver Maqsud of Kashan — arguably the most ambitious textile object in the early-modern world.

One of the two Ardabil Carpets is now in the Victoria and Albert Museum in London; the other is in the Los Angeles County Museum of Art — the pair split between the two institutions after a complicated provenance trail through Iranian, Ottoman, and English hands.

Reza Abbasi, the leading court painter of Abbas's Isfahan, shifted Persian miniature painting toward naturalistic single-figure compositions — a young dervish reading, a courtier reclining — breaking from the dense narrative compositions of earlier Persian book painting.

Persian carpet and miniature traditions flowed directly into Mughal art: two Tabriz painters, Mir Sayyid Ali and Abd al-Samad, migrated to Delhi and became founding figures of Mughal painting, and the Persian-language literary tradition dominated Mughal culture from Babur to Aurangzeb.
subtitle: the silk monopoly, Armenian merchants at New Julfa, and the Ardabil Carpet

title: The Long Decline
bullets: Safi I's first act as Shah was purging Abbas's senior commanders and officials — including the great ghulam general Imam Quli Khan and his entire family — replacing competent veterans with younger loyalists whose capability was untested.
In 1638, the Ottomans under Murad IV recaptured Baghdad after a forty-day siege, ending Iranian control of the Shia holy cities of Najaf and Karbala for good.

The Treaty of Zuhab signed in 1639 fixed the Ottoman-Safavid border so precisely that it is essentially the modern Iran-Iraq boundary — a line that has survived the collapse of both empires, the British creation of Iraq, the Iran-Iraq War, and the American invasion.
Abbas II's recapture of Kandahar in 1649, defeating the Mughal prince Aurangzeb in three successive battles, was the last major Safavid military success.

The harem succession system — Abbas the Great had confined all royal princes inside the Isfahan palace, ending provincial apprenticeships — produced each generation of Safavid kings less prepared for rule than the last.

The French jeweler Jean Chardin, whose ten-volume account of his 1672–77 Iranian travels is one of the most detailed European descriptions of the Safavid Empire, found surface magnificence concealing a system visibly rotting: incompetent Shah, corrupt officials, undisciplined army.

Gurgin Khan, a Georgian Christian convert serving as Safavid governor of Kandahar, conducted brutal campaigns against the Sunni Ghilzai Pashtuns in the early 1700s, provoking a revolt under chieftain Mir Wais Hotak that killed him and established an independent Afghan emirate in 1709.

At Gulnabad on March 8, 1722, a Safavid imperial force outnumbering the Afghan invaders two-to-one was routed in a single day: the guns were captured, the lines collapsed, and the road to Isfahan was open.
subtitle: palace purges, the Treaty of Zuhab, and the Afghan revolt that ended everything

title: The Afghan Catastrophe and Nader's Restoration
bullets: The six-and-a-half-month Afghan siege of Isfahan in 1722 killed an estimated 80,000 to 200,000 people — a third to half the urban population — by starvation, with contemporary accounts describing the population eating dogs, cats, leather, and tree bark before surrender.

On October 23, 1722, Sultan Husayn rode out of Isfahan, removed his royal aigrette, and placed the Safavid crown on Mir Mahmud Hotak's head, formally handing the oldest surviving Iranian dynasty to a Pashtun warlord in his twenties.

By 1725, Mahmud — mentally ill, possibly from syphilis — ordered the massacre of most of the surviving Safavid royal family and Iranian nobility, executing perhaps 3,000 people in a single afternoon at the Isfahan palace.

While Afghans held the center, Russia seized the Caspian coast and the Ottomans occupied Azerbaijan and western Iran; the two foreign powers signed a treaty in 1724 formally dividing the conquered Iranian territories between themselves.

Nader Shah Afshar — illiterate Afshar tribesman, teenage kidnapping victim of Uzbek slavers, former bandit-mercenary — became Tahmasp II's general in 1726 and within three years had expelled the Afghans, defeating the Hotakis at Damghan and Murche-Khort in 1729.

In 1739 Nader invaded Mughal India, defeated Emperor Muhammad Shah at the Battle of Karnal, and occupied Delhi for fifty-seven days, looting the Peacock Throne, the Koh-i-Noor diamond, and enough cash to suspend taxation in Iran for three years.

In March 1736 on the Mughan plain, Nader formally deposed the infant Safavid Abbas III and proclaimed himself Shah, ending 235 years of the dynasty; but when he proposed reclassifying Twelver Shia as a fifth Sunni school to normalize relations with the Ottomans, the Iranian clergy simply refused, and he could not make them comply.

The dynasty died in 1736; the Twelver Shia state, the clerical hierarchy, the shrine networks at Mashhad and Qom, and the cultural identity that distinguishes Iranians from every Sunni neighbor survived — and survive still.
subtitle: the Afghan siege of Isfahan, Nader Shah's restoration, and the Shia identity that outlasted them all
